issue_title: wrong json created for AWS::Logs::Destination
issue_body:
`logs.Destination` doesnt generate the correct json CloudFormation expects since [its sublcassing the wrong thing]( https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/blob/c75f4e2186bc06385cfbad98101e5163aeee581e/troposphere/logs.py#L5). should be `AWSObject` instead of `AWSProperty`

attempting to create the stack throws this exception
```python
botocore.exceptions.ClientError: An error occurred (ValidationError) when calling the CreateStack operation: Invalid template resource property 'DestinationName'
```

bad json output
```json
"MyLogDestination": {
    "DestinationName": "destination-name",
    "DestinationPolicy": "destination-policy-arn",
    "RoleArn": "role-arn",
    "TargetArn": "target-arn",
    "Type": "AWS::Logs::Destination"
}
```

according to the [docs](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-logs-destination.html), the output should look like this:
```json
"MyLogDestination": {
    "Properties": {
        "DestinationName": "destination-name",
        "DestinationPolicy": "destination-policy-arn",
        "RoleArn": "role-arn",
        "TargetArn": "target-arn"
    },
    "Type": "AWS::Logs::Destination"
}
```

issue_title: Request: being able to provide Thrift generate options to *_thrift_library()
issue_body:
Right now you can specify the gen_options as part of gen.thrift in the global pants.ini config:

```
[gen.thrift]
deps: ["3rdparty:thrift"]
gen_options: private-members,generated_annotations=undated
strict: False
```

Or you can provide/override them when compiling/packacking a particular target: 
`--gen-thrift-gen-options=private-members,generated_annotations=undated`

Say you have a particular target that wants to add more generate options, you need to use the latter approach by specifying the command line options, which impacts all thrift libraries in the dependency chain as well.

It would be nice to be able to do that as part of the actual *_thrift_library() definition in the BUILD file so that it impacts just that target. This is also less error prone when you have multiple targets using those thrift files.

issue_title: Remove the need to specify scala tools in BUILD.tools
issue_body:
All (?) other tools now have default classspaths ([via](https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/2778/)), but because the scala tools do not have a reasonable global default, they currently need to be manually set in BUILD.tools.

https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/2891/ added basic support for scala platforms. This ticket should deal with improving the scala platform support to allow for configuring _only_ the scala platform version (ie `2.11`), and having the compiler/library/etc use default values for those platforms (ie `2.11.7` being the most recent release of the `2.11` platform).

After this ticket, it should be possible to build Java code in a brand new pants install without configuring scala tools.

issue_title: Port the python pipeline to the v2 engine
issue_body:
This is an umbrella ticket to cover porting the python backend away from the v1 `Task` model and into v2 `@rules`: it is expected that it will have a lot of blockers, but also that this ticket itself should be broken up and accomplished in phases from the bottom up.

Creating a "python only" install of pants (according to the instructions on #4782) results in the following {{./pants --explain test}} output:
```
Goal Execution Order:

gen -> pyprep -> test

Goal [TaskRegistrar->Task] Order:

gen [thrift-py->ApacheThriftPyGen_gen_thrift_py]
pyprep [interpreter->SelectInterpreter_pyprep_interpreter, requirements->ResolveRequirements_pyprep_requirements, sources->GatherSources_pyprep_sources]
test [test-prep-command->RunTestPrepCommand_test_test_prep_command, test->NoopTest_test, pytest-prep->PytestPrep_test_pytest_prep, pytest->PytestRun_test_pytest]
```
IE: with no JVM dependencies, the bootstrap phase disappears. There is a lie here though: generating `antlr` sources for a `python_antlr_library` requires the antlr JVM tool... and that currently introduces a dependency on the JVM backend and the `bootstrap` goal.

----

Known components and blockers:
[ ] Get an alpha version of #4769 in place to represent the root requests for the v2 pipeline
[ ] Do enough of #4535 to flesh out what the Target model with look like with `Fields` (and to determine whether "fields" is the right UX)
[ ] Porting the `test.pytest` and `pyprep.*` tasks, initially without codegen 
[ ] Figuring out the `antlr` issue mentioned above and porting codegen

issue_title: Rationalize jar_publish repo confs config
issue_body:
Today each defined publish repo has a list of configs.  This list is - for the most part - unused, and the default(jar), sources and javadoc artifacts are all always published.  The list only allows for adding in the 'changelog' to the list of published artifacts.

The relevant template is here - note the publications guarded by `lib.publications.*` guards: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/tasks/templates/ivy_resolve/ivy.mustache#L32
This bit of code though unconditionally adds the sources and javadoc publications (confs):
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/tasks/jar_publish.py#L719-724

So, in the end, the user-supplied confs for a repo can only turn on the 'changelog' publication and cannot turn off sources or javadoc publication.

I think the current forced publication of jar + sources + javadoc is correct, with changelog optional, its just the configuration that needs to change.  If the repo confs list was replaced with a changelog=True/False that would be enough.

issue_title: git scm server_url origin check is maybe too loose
issue_body:
We're working on setting up an alternate origin for one of our repos at Twitter, and an engineer decided to name the new origin "`temp_origin`":

```
[areitz@atla-bgy-03-sr3 source_4 ((bcfc917...))]$  git remote -v
origin	https://git.twitter.biz/ro/source (fetch)
origin	https://git.twitter.biz/ro/source (push)
temp_origin	https://git.twitter.biz/ro/source-ci (fetch)
temp_origin	https://git.twitter.biz/ro/source-ci (push)
```

However, doing so leads to this error when `scm.server_url()` is invoked (where `scm` is an instance of `Git`:

```
Exception message: Unable to find origin remote amongst: origin	https://git.twitter.biz/ro/source (fetch)
origin	https://git.twitter.biz/ro/source (push)
temp_origin	https://git.twitter.biz/ro/source-ci (fetch)
temp_origin	https://git.twitter.biz/ro/source-ci (push)
```

Our current thinking is that the check for `origin` should be more strict -- maybe something like `/\borigin\b`. I plan on coming up with a patch for this later today.

issue_title: Page Numbers (feature request)
issue_body:
It would be incredibly useful to me to be able to predict what page I'm on. That is, I add some text to a document and can have the program figure out how long the document is in pages. 

Or maybe I can ask "what page is the element at index X on" which would be even better. 

My usecase for this is that I want to generate a book and, somehow, I have to create a table of contents and an index. Both of these require that I know what page something will be on eventually.

Louison

issue_title: Best practice for fixing failed tests?
issue_body:
In the latest build, five tests failed. Two of these have been failing for a while, and are due to unusual spellings:

```python
# Changing "Hgwy" -> "Hwy" fixes the failure
ADDRESS:     b'12631 Imperial Hgwy Santa Fe CA 90670'
pred:        ('AddressNumber', 'StreetName', 'StreetName', 'PlaceName', 'PlaceName', 'StateName', 'ZipCode')
true:        ('AddressNumber', 'StreetName', 'StreetNamePostType', 'PlaceName', 'PlaceName', 'StateName', 'ZipCode')
```

```python
# Changing "Pakway" -> "Parkway" fixes the failure
ADDRESS:     b'1341 Northeast Orenco Station Pakway, Hillsboro, OR 97124'
pred:        ['AddressNumber', 'StreetName', 'StreetName', 'StreetName', 'PlaceName', 'PlaceName', 'StateName', 'ZipCode']
true:        ['AddressNumber', 'StreetName', 'StreetName', 'StreetName', 'StreetName', 'PlaceName', 'StateName', 'ZipCode']
```

In one of the three new failures, it seems that the new training data has weighted the model in the wrong direction:

```python
ADDRESS:     b'Hwys 16 And 63 North, Spring Valley, MN 55975'
pred:        ['SubaddressType', 'SubaddressIdentifier', 'SubaddressType', 'SubaddressIdentifier', 'PlaceName', 'PlaceName', 'PlaceName', 'StateName', 'ZipCode']
true:        ['StreetName', 'StreetName', 'IntersectionSeparator', 'StreetName', 'StreetName', 'PlaceName', 'PlaceName', 'StateName', 'ZipCode']
```

(The last batch of training data contained lots of examples of unusual subaddresses that appeared at the front of the string.)

These cases seem like __proper failures__. We should try to tweak the model so that it passes these tests.

In two of the new failures, however, the "true" output strikes me as debatable:

```python
# Floors can be either subaddresses or occupancy types, depending on the context)
ADDRESS:     b'431 Marietta St NW Floor 3'
pred:        ('AddressNumber', 'StreetName', 'StreetNamePostType', 'SubaddressIdentifier', 'SubaddressType', 'SubaddressIdentifier')
true:        ('AddressNumber', 'StreetName', 'StreetNamePostType', 'StreetNamePostDirectional', 'OccupancyType', 'OccupancyIdentifier')
```

```python
# Since 123456789 is not a valid zip code, both `OccupancyIdentifier` and `ZipCode` seem like reasonable guesses to me
ADDRESS:     b'123 W Hastings St 123456789'
pred:        ('AddressNumber', 'StreetNamePreDirectional', 'StreetName', 'StreetNamePostType', 'OccupancyIdentifier')
true:        ('AddressNumber', 'StreetNamePreDirectional', 'StreetName', 'StreetNamePostType', 'ZipCode')
```

These cases seem like __improper failures__. We should tweak the tests to be less ambiguous.

Whether we're dealing with "proper" or "improper" failures, __what's our standard procedure for addressing failed tests?__ For failures that seem like true mistakes, my instinct is to hunt around for valid addresses that resemble the failure and add them to the training data. For failures where the parse succeeds but its output is debatably correct, my instinct is to remove the tests and add less ambiguous real-world examples that get at the meat of the test. 

If we have a policy, it might make sense to add it to the documentation in case someone creates a new build and needs to deal with failed tests.

issue_title: Using 'id' consistently in API responses
issue_body:
`id` and `pk` are used seemingly interchangeably in responses from the REST API , which is kind of confusing.

There seems to be a preference in the JS code for `id`, as when a response uses `pk`, it is often renamed to `id`. `pk` is also hints at the underlying database, while `id` is a little more abstract.

issue_title: clicking through items during import should cause a 'loading' state

there is currently no user feedback when clicking items in the import UI, and we allow the user to click other things while a request is pending. Note that the URL is changing, but the visible UI is not:

Navigate through a large channel, such as KA English

re #3717

issue_title: v4.0.0 and higher: AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'proxy_info' when attempting to connect to DFP using a service account.
issue_body:
## Expected behavior
A service account with suitable privileges assigned should be able to connect to DFP services

## Actual behavior
Attempts to connect to DFP with a valid service account result in Python `AttributeError`, apparently because `GoogleServiceAccountClient.Refresh.proxy_config` returns `None`.

## Information
Some of my code started failing following `googleads` upgrade. To isolate the behavior to `googleads`, I composed the following simple test case, to connect to the DFP network service (based mainly on copy and paste from https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/tree/master/examples/dfp)
```
from googleads import dfp
from googleads import oauth2

# OAuth2 credential information. In a real application, you'd probably be
# pulling these values from a credential storage.
SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL = 'fake-pretend@developer.gserviceaccount.com'
KEY_FILE = '/secret/gcskey.p12'

# DFP API information.
APPLICATION_NAME = 'fake-pretend'


def dfp_api_client():

    oauth2_client = oauth2.GoogleServiceAccountClient(
            oauth2.GetAPIScope('dfp'), SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL, KEY_FILE)
    dfp_client = dfp.DfpClient(oauth2_client, APPLICATION_NAME)
    return dfp_client


def main(client):
    # directly from https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/examples/dfp/v201602/network_service/get_all_networks.py
    # Initialize appropriate service.
    network_service = client.GetService('NetworkService', version='v201602')

    # Get all networks that you have access to with the current login credentials.
    networks = network_service.getAllNetworks()

    # Display results.
    for network in networks:
        print ('Network with network code \'%s\' and display name \'%s\' was found.'
             % (network['networkCode'], network['displayName']))

    print '\nNumber of results found: %s' % len(networks)

if __name__ == '__main__':
  # Initialize client object.
  dfp_client = dfp_api_client()
  main(dfp_client)
```
This works entirely as expected at v3.15.0, but fails with the following traceback with v4.0.0 and higher:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "api-test.py", line 37, in <module>
    dfp_client = dfp_api_client()# dfp.DfpClient.LoadFromStorage()
  File "api-test.py", line 16, in dfp_api_client
    oauth2.GetAPIScope('dfp'), SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL, KEY_FILE)
  File "/Users/thomas/.virtualenvs/tmp-1e8b453a8dbebace/lib/python2.7/site-packages/googleads/oauth2.py", line 203, in __init__
    self.Refresh()
  File "/Users/thomas/.virtualenvs/tmp-1e8b453a8dbebace/lib/python2.7/site-packages/googleads/oauth2.py", line 236, in Refresh
    proxy_info=self.proxy_config.proxy_info,
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'proxy_info'
```

## Steps to reproduce
1. Install googleads 3.15.0
2. Attempt connection to DFP API using a service account, which should work
3. upgrade to googleads 4.x
4. Attempt connection again

issue_title: Make a release of Python-OMX
issue_body:
[ActivitySim](https://github.com/synthicity/activitysim) needs to read OMX files. Based on feedback from the community we'd like to have OMX available as a standalone Python library installable via [pip](https://pip.pypa.io/en/latest/) and [conda](http://conda.pydata.org/) (on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi) and [binstar](https://binstar.org/), respectively). This kind of release is required so we can easily install the OMX library for things like automated testing and so it can be listed as an explicit dependency of ActivitySim.

Related to making a release, I'd recommend splitting the directories for each OMX library into separate repositories. That's not strictly necessary, but it has some conveniences:

- users can install omx directly from GitHub via pip
- tagged releases of Python code don't include the other languages
- maintenance and development branches for one language don't include the other languages

As an example, the official GitHub API wrappers for different languages are kept in separate repositories: https://github.com/octokit.

Whether you decide to split the the directories or not, I'm happy to help with the infrastructure for a release. We'll need this available soon for ActivitySim development and testing. (Though not until after the New Year.)

You may also be interested in the changes I made for the proposed integration of OMX with ActivitySim: https://github.com/synthicity/activitysim/pull/7. The changes included PEP8 compliance, switch to [pytest](http://pytest.org/latest/) for running unit tests, and additional test coverage.

issue_title: Current PyPI version does not support Python 3

issue_body:
Both [here](https://github.com/juju/charm-tools) and on [Launchpad](https://launchpad.net/charm-tools), one of the first things a user who stops by will read are install instructions that use the PPA.  Our [official documentation](https://jujucharms.com/docs/stable/tools-charm-tools) has been changed to reflect the fact that the PPA has been deprecated as debs are no longer supported and snaps are their replacement.

Two issues, really: 1) we need to remove the PPAs from Launchpad and GitHub, and 2) we need snap [install instructions for non-Xenial users](https://github.com/juju/docs/issues/1649) to update our documentation as well as to finish the fix in part 1.

issue_title: Deleting a column in a lightweight transaction raises a SyntaxException
issue_body:
Hi,

When using `iff()` and updating a column to `None`, the engine tries to execute an invalid query, ressembling this: `UPDATE my_column_family SET  WHERE [...]`. If other columns are updated, the update query is correctly executed, as well as the `DELETE column FROM...`. This also occurs when performing updates in a batch.

It does work as expected if `iff()` isn't used.

To re-create the bug (based on examples from the doc):
```python
User.objects(id=1).iff(name='test').update(name=None)
```

issue_title: Change network.Zone to network.SecurityZone and more fully implement it
n_jobs for refitting

After running a grid search grid search , it would be nice to use more of the cores on the master for the final refit step.  The n_jobs parameter exists for compatibility, but it is currently no-op. issue_title: Not possible to get all results of a search
issue_body: The `--limit` parameter doesn't allow the user to set the maximum number of search results to be *unlimited*. It might be useful to allow the user to override all limits on the number of results since this is possible with the Python client by setting the limit to `None`.

issue_title: Parse DriverStatus returned by info()
issue_body:
The info() method should return something usable programmatically. Currently it returns a dictionary containing the `DriverStatus`, a key that contains a list of "stuff".

Parsing this DriverStatus key is a nightmare:
```
[['\x08Role', 'primary'], ['\x08Strategy', 'spread'], ['\x08Filters', 'health, port, dependency, affinity, constraint'], ['\x08Nodes', '2'], ['bf12', 'bf12:2375'], [' └ Status', 'Healthy'], [' └ Containers', '2'], [' └ Reserved CPUs', '0 / 33'], [' └ Reserved Memory', '0 B / 132.1 GiB'], [' └ Labels', 'executiondriver=native-0.2, kernelversion=3.19.0-25-generic, operatingsystem=Ubuntu 14.04.3 LTS, storagedriver=aufs'], ['bf13', 'bf13:2375'], [' └ Status', 'Healthy'], [' └ Containers', '2'], [' └ Reserved CPUs', '0 / 33'], [' └ Reserved Memory', '0 B / 132.1 GiB'], [' └ Labels', 'executiondriver=native-0.2, kernelversion=3.19.0-25-generic, operatingsystem=Ubuntu 14.04.3 LTS, storagedriver=aufs']]
```

I can understand that users of the API cannot expect any field to be available, depending on the docker version they are querying, but at least generate nested dictionaries for the nodes and remove extraneous characters.

issue_title: Requirements Update
issue_body: It seems like `draw_chimera` requires numpy and matplotlib, but these dependencies are not listed in `requirements.txt`.  That said, both are fairly heavy dependencies for a simply graph layout.  It might make sense for this package to provide the `chimera_layout` function, which then can be combined with any viz tool (e.g. matplotlib) to provide the drawing dependencies.

issue_title: Bug in to_junction_tree() method in MarkovModel.
issue_body:
Bug in converting the following Markov Model to Junction Tree.

```python
from pgmpy.models import MarkovModel
from pgmpy.factors import Factor
import numpy as np
mm = MarkovModel()
mm.add_edges_from([('a','a1'),('b','b1'),('a1','c1'),('b1','c1'),('a','b'),('a1','b1')])
factor_dict = {}
factor_dict[1] = Factor(('a','b'), [2,2],np.random.rand(4))
factor_dict[2] = Factor(('a','a1'), [2,2], np.random.rand(4))
factor_dict[3] = Factor(('b','b1'), [2,2], np.random.rand(4))
factor_dict[4] = Factor(('a1','b1','c1'),[2,2,3], np.random.rand(12))
mm.add_factors(*[factor for key,factor in factor_dict.items()])
jt = mm.to_junction_tree()
```

issue_title: Codegenerator doesn't support multiple output arrays whose size is determined by "IVI dance"
issue_body:
Codegenerator doesn't support multiple output arrays whose size is determined by "IVI dance".
Imagine the following function, which is actually not in my imagination at all:

```
ViStatus _VI_FUNC niScope_GetFrequencyResponse (
    ViSession vi,
    ViConstString channelName,
    ViInt32 bufferSize,
    ViReal64 frequencies[],
    ViReal64 amplitudes[],
    ViReal64 phases[],
    ViInt32* numberOfFrequencies);
```

...where frequencies, amplitudes, phases are output arrays of the same size, and size is determined by IVI-dance.

Turns out our generator doesn't support it (there's an assert). What we need is logic that says "it's ok to have multiple output arrays whose size is determined via IVI-dance as long as they are all the same size (aka use the same parameter to pass the size).

    getattr(module, method_name))
AttributeError: module 'txaio.aio' has no attribute 'using_twisted'
```

Have you guys seen this problem before? Any idea what the issue is?

Below is the code:

- `runners`
|-- `__init__.py`
|-- `asyncio.py`
|-- `twisted.py`

`__init__.py`
```python
# coding=utf-8
from __future__ import unicode_literals

import json
from argparse import ArgumentParser
from importlib import import_module
from os import environ

import txaio

__author__ = 'Adam Jorgensen <adam.jorgensen@opendna.ai>'


def generate_parser() -> ArgumentParser:
    """
    Generates an ArgumentParser instance for use by the `main` function
    defined in this module

    :return:
    """
    parser = ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument(
        '-c', '--component',
        default=environ.get('CROSSBAR_COMPONENT'),
        help='Fully-qualified path to a Component class'
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        '-u', '--url',
        default=environ.get('CROSSBAR_URL'),
        help=''
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        '-r', '--realm',
        default=environ.get('CROSSBAR_REALM'),
        help=''
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        '-e', '--extra-file',
        default=environ.get('CROSSBAR_EXTRAS_FILE'),
        help='Path to JSON file of data to be supplied to the '
             'Component via the config __init__ parameter'
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        '-s', '--use-ssl', dest='ssl', type=bool,
        default=environ.get('CROSSBAR_USE_SSL', False),
        help=''
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        '-l', '--loglevel', dest='log_level', default='info',
        help=''
    )
    # TODO: Add an argument for serializers
    return parser


def get_class(fully_qualified_class_path: str) -> type:
    """
    Returns a class given an input fully-qualified class path E.g.
    a.b.c.d.ClassName

    :param fully_qualified_class_path:
    :return:
    """
    module_name, class_name = fully_qualified_class_path.rsplit('.', 1)
    imported_module = import_module(module_name)
    return getattr(imported_module, class_name)


def main(application_runner_class: type):
    """
    Given an input CrossBar ApplicationRunner class, runs a Component
    defined using input from either the command-line or environment variables
    with parameters determined by said input.

    :param application_runner_class:
    :return:
    """
    parser = generate_parser()
    args = parser.parse_args()
    component = get_class(args.component)
    extra = None
    if args.extra_file is not None:
        extra = json.load(open(args.extra_file))
    runner = application_runner_class(extra=extra, **{
        key: value
        for key, value in vars(args).items()
        if key not in ('component', 'extra_file', 'log_level')
    })
    txaio.start_logging(level=args.log_level)
    return runner.run(component)
```

from runners import main

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main(ApplicationRunner)
```

`twisted.py`

from autobahn.twisted.wamp import ApplicationRunner

from runners import main

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main(ApplicationRunner)
```

issue_title: ApplicationRunner should not auto_reconnect after failed login
issue_body:
When auto_reconnect is enabled, the ApplicationRunner does not know whether or not it ever successfully connected in the first place. As a result, connections lost due to incorrect login information are constantly re-attempted with the same incorrect information.

A check that prevents this exists in Autobahn JS: 
https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-js/blob/master/lib/connection.js#L349

issue_title: DatasetReaders no longer have any tqdm output on read
issue_body: This is unfortunate if you're reading a lot of data, because you just sit there not knowing how long it's going to take.  Probably the fix is to change the `lazy=False` case in `DatasetReader.read()` to use `tqdm` instead of `ensure_list`.  I'll do this soon unless someone tells me there's a better way to do this.

issue_title: six version is pinned to 0.9.0
issue_body: Because the `requirements.txt` sets `six==0.9.0`, projects that have multiple dependencies on six that use only a lower bound may fail to run because a newer version 0.10.0 might be installed as a result of processing some other dependency and pip is apparently not clever enough to figure all this out on its own.  Any specific reason why the requirement couldn't be `six>=0.9.0` ?

issue_title: Separate "Scratch" Directories for Different Purposes
issue_body:
TL;DR: I'd like to be able to choose different locations for the different types of files stored in the music21 "scratch" directory.

The "scratch" directory is used for two very different things:

* Creating temporary files while preparing output.
* Storing pickled versions of previously-imported output.

The first of these is the definition of "temporary" files, and they could be removed virtually right away after they're created. Certainly they *should* be removed periodically.

The second of these is not at all temporary, since it's only useful if the files are *not* removed periodically (certainly at much longer periods than the temporary files).

Because music21 puts these in the same place, it's more difficult to manage the different roles these files play. For a desktop user, it's not a big deal. For deployment on a server, I want to put some of these files in ``/tmp`` and some of them in ``/var/cache``, and they require different (SELinux) security labels.

For these reasons, I'd like to be able to set different directories for the different types of files.

(Note this is indeed low priority).

issue_title: dump.to_sql should detect sql engine problems before starting to download
issue_body:
### reproduction steps
* start scraping some data using dump.to_sql but with invalid SQL engine setting

#### expected
* milliseconds after running should fail with an error regarding failure to connect to SQL server

#### actual
* pipeline starts running
* might download some data, do some stuff
* only when it reaches the dump.to_sql it will fail

### notes
* the more general fix would be to allow processors a `pre` step - before data is streamed
* not sure if such an option exists today or not
* then, dump.to_sql will try to connect to SQL on this `pre` step

issue_title: New & safer mechanism to generate DSC module output file names
issue_body: With implementation of new file/task cache system in SoS (that uses sqlite database), the DSC module output file naming mechanism is no longer bullet proof as a result of occasional database transaction error & recovery. I need to rethink and reimplement the module output name handling in DSC -- both correctness and efficiency are important and that was why it initially relies on the DAG that SoS builds. Now I need to make it more robust.

issue_title: Deis should check if container's port is accepting connections before adding it to the pool
issue_body:
Sometimes you need to run commands to setup your application before you run the web server. E.g. you may want to run `./manage.py syncdb` in a django app to apply database migrations before you run  `gunicorn`.

Deis should check if the exposed port of the container accepts connections, before adding the container to the pool.  Otherwise the container gets into the pool, router sends traffic to it and nginx returns 502 (bad gateway) because no server listens to the exposed port of a second or two.

Is this something that can go into [IsPublishableApp](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/publisher/server/publisher.go#L104)?

(Apologies if this has being already reported. I've this memory but I cannot find the issue)

issue_title: [meta] Deis Operator UX Theme
issue_body:
Milestone: v2.0-beta2

## Installation / Configuration

As an operator, I should be able to quickly configure object storage
consistently across Deis Workflow when running `helm install deis/workflow-...`

Most of the work is described tracked in: https://github.com/deis/deis/issues/4966

Scenarios to test and validate:
* On-cluster: minio, default
* Off-cluster: AWS
    * Bucket in us-east-1 (s3 us-standard)
    * Bucket in us-west-2 (s3 regional endpoint)
* Off-cluster: GCS w/ S3 compat mode
    * us-central1-x
    * us-east1-x
* [X] Bucket for database backups `slug_bucket`
* [X] Bucket for slugs `slug_bucket`
* [X] Bucket for registry `registry_bucket`

## Recoverability

Operators need to have confidence that they can recover from cluster disasters.
We need to test and validate cluster recovery from off-cluster object storage
to working platform.

Reproducible Helm charts: https://github.com/deis/charts/issues/199

Given a set of buckets, used by a previous installation of the platform. An
Operator should be able to quickly recover their instance of Deis Workflow.

Assumes a brand-new k8s cluster, with no conflicting or lingering application
namespaces.

This should be tested on both S3 and GCS-backed clusters.

## Champagne

Learn by doing! We need to host our own applications. First two applications
are deis slack inviter and workflow-manager API backend.

https://github.com/deis/deployment/issues/8

## Delivery Process

@sgoings details here!

issue_title: Consider a new default scheduler
issue_body: Deis now has support for Docker Swarm, Apache Mesos, and Google Kubernetes as application schedulers. With the known limitations of fleet (primarily, not being a resource-aware scheduler), we should investigate using a different scheduler as our default.

issue_title: Add `globus logout` command
issue_body:
The inverse of `globus login`.
This can't touch the consent in Globus Auth, but it should invalidate all of your tokens by revoking them, and scrub them from the config file.

issue_title: RenewingAuthorizer implementation is in violation of OAuth2 spec
issue_body:
This is not very high priority due to the implementation of Globus Auth. In our case, this violation makes no difference. However, future changes which expect this to be correct would be prone to issues.

While reading the OAuth2 spec (again) for other work, I noticed this in [section 6](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-6):
> The authorization server MAY issue a new refresh token, in which case
> the client MUST discard the old refresh token and replace it with the
> new refresh token.

Our refresh code does not treat the refresh token at all:

https://github.com/globus/globus-sdk-python/blob/85f09d5ec979bd606f908e1001bbaa55e61980aa/globus_sdk/authorizers/renewing.py#L89-L107

No change to `on_refresh` is necessary, as we pass complete token data.

I'll open a CLI issue to fix its `on_refresh` handler.

issue_title: Matching between requested headers and received headers.
issue_labels: [{'id': 889922027, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4ODk5MjIwMjc=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/comp:%20p2p', 'name': 'comp: p2p', 'color': 'bfd4f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 815420353, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4MTU0MjAzNTM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/comp:%20trinity', 'name': 'comp: trinity', 'color': 'bfd4f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 983616278, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5ODM2MTYyNzg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/type:%20RFC%20/%20discussion%20/%20question', 'name': 'type: RFC / discussion / question', 'color': 'eeeeee', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

While running trinity against the current master @ 3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f I'm seeing a lot of these exceptions:

```python
   ERROR  07-17 11:10:22  base_events  Task exception was never retrieved
future: <Task finished coro=<BaseHeaderChainSyncer.sync() done, defined at /Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/chain.py:156> exception=ValidationError('Cannot end the ThroughputTracker without starting it',)>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/chain.py", line 169, in sync
    await self._sync(peer)
  File "/Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/chain.py", line 230, in _sync
    head_number = await self._process_headers(peer, headers)
  File "/Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/chain.py", line 376, in _process_headers
    BlockPartEnum.body,
  File "/Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/chain.py", line 451, in _download_block_parts
    self._get_peer_stats(peer, part_name).complete_work(work_size)
  File "/Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/utils.py", line 84, in complete_work
    raise ValidationError("Cannot end the ThroughputTracker without starting it")
eth.exceptions.ValidationError: Cannot end the ThroughputTracker without starting it
```

In trying to understand where these are coming from, I'm working to understand the syncing logic and want to be sure I have it correct.


### What I think is going on (please confirm)

- [`p2p.chain.BaseHeaderChainSyncer._sync#L205`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/chain.py#L205) requests headers from the chosen `peer`
- [`p2p.chain.BaseHeaderChainSyncer._fetch_missing_headers#L253`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/chain.py#L253) issues a request to the peer for some headers.
- [`p2p.chain.BaseHeaderChainSyncer._fetch_missing_headers#L256`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/chain.py#L256) waits for new headers to show up in the `_new_headers` queue.
- When the new headers show up, [`p2p.chain.BaseHeaderChainSyncer._handle_block_headers`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/chain.py#L276) puts the new headers in the `_new_headers` queue.

So, IIUC, if **any** peer sends us a `BlockHeaders` message, the new headers will end up in the `_new_headers` queue and the `headers` returned in [`_fetch_missing_headers`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/chain.py#L256) are not guaranteed to originate from the peer they were requested from.

Alternate explanation of what I'm trying to understand.

1. We request headers from peer `A`
2. Peer `B` sends us a `BlockHeaders` message before peer `A` responds (we didn't ask for them to)
3. The headers from peer `B` end up in the queue.
4. We treat the headers received from peer `B` as if they were sent from peer `A`.

If this is the case then I believe we have a systemic problem that we need to solve.

### How we can solve it (maybe)

My understanding is that there is no `id` for these types of requests so we cannot do `id` based linking between requests and responses.

Without an `id` based way to link requests and responses, I think we have to do it in a probabilistic manner.

1. instead of a single queue for headers, lets use a queue per peer.
2. use the [`PeerPoolSubscriber.deregister_peer`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/peer.py#L706) API to clear out stale queues.
3. maintain list of *header requests* that are awaiting responses.
4. on receipt of `BlockHeaders` message, we only put it in the queue if it has a corresponding *header request* which is satisfied by the headers contained in the message.

Defining *header request*:

- A *header request* would be defined as the requesting parameters in the `GetBlockHeaders` message.  
- A match would be defined as a non-empty set of headers which is a subset of the full set of headers that could be generated by the request.

Any received headers that cannot be correlated to a specific peer and header request should be logged and discarded.

issue_title: Missing `account_exists` implementation prevents execution of sharding transactions
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

Executing sharding transactions currently does not work with the FlatTrieBackend as it doesn't implement `account_exists`.

Note that it is not related to account creation transactions per se (as I assumed earlier), normal transactions are affected in the same way.

### How can it be fixed

Either implement `account_exists` or don't use it. It is called in some SELFDESTUCT (e.g. https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/362266c323c83a851f7dff0a2a59796abaa3f318/evm/logic/system.py#L64) and some CALL (e.g. https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/362266c323c83a851f7dff0a2a59796abaa3f318/evm/logic/call.py#L287) opcodes. It changes from fork to fork though and I'm not sure if this is still needed, especially as at least one one motivation seems to have been the dust clean up in Spurious Dragon. Also, it seems to be used for gas calculation only which will be different with stateless clients anyway.

It is also called indirectly via `touch_account` for every message, but this can unproblematically be replaced by setting nonce/balance/code to zero unless they are non-zero.

Implementing `account_exists` is probably the easier solution (with `check_if_branch_exist` in py-trie), but I don't like this as much because I can't see at the moment why it is needed.

issue_body:
### What was wrong?

In the following integration tests that test custom registrations, our method of testing is a bit stateful since it registers then unregisters coders for the custom data type.  If one test fails, that could lead to a confusing failure in another test when the registry complains that a registration already exists for the custom type:

https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/tests/test_integration/test_custom_registrations.py#L69-L99

### How can it be fixed?

We could modify those tests to use a fixture registry instance that is created per test run.  If we want to continue to do true integration testing via `encode_single` and `decode_single`, we will probably need to modify the API to support use of a custom registry instance.

issue_title: ``deactivate`` adds miniconda/bin from path
issue_body:
I have just installed miniconda3 for testing purposes. When installing I've said **not** to add the path to miniconda3/bin to the ``.bashrc`` file.

I've created an environment and activated.

    source miniconda3/bin/activate my_env
    discarding /home/user/miniconda3/bin from PATH  # which I don't have
    prepending /home/user/miniconda3/envs/my_env/bin to PATH

When I deactivated it it told me 

    discarding /home/user/miniconda3/envs/conda_hetdex/bin from PATH

but it silently added the path to miniconda3/bin, messing up my environment.

Since I asked not to add miniconda to my path, I would call it a bug.
Upon activation, conda should remember whether miniconda3/bin is on the path. When deactivating it should read it only if it was already there. I guess that it would be something similar to the way PS1 is saved in the CONDA_OLD_PS1 environment variable.

issue_title: `conda clean -p` partially deletes root Python installation on 32 bit Windows
issue_body:
There appears to be an issue with how `conda clean -p` detects whether or not a package is in use. It attempts to delete the root Python installation in the `pkgs` directory even though it is in use and then silently ignores file locking issues that come up when a file handle is held by a running process. Installing an older bugfix release and then reinstalling the latest one can be used to restore the root install.

We're seeing this with Python 2.7.11 build 2 on Windows Server 2008 and Windows 8.

issue_title: Cycles in conda-build build and run dependencies.
issue_body:
Hi Michael. If you clone https://github.com/mingwandroid/conda/conda-recipes and look in cyclic-test, you'll see a very simplified version of how the R suite of packages are setup (in particular, r itself, r-base the r-recommended meta-package and the r-recommended sub-packages). I renamed r to rust to avoid any conflicts too.

There are a few obvious cycles here. r-recommended loops back immediately to r (for no reason I can see, I expect this can be dropped without any issue at all), then each of the r-recommended sub-packages have a run-dependency back to r also. Some of the r-recommended sub-packages depend on each other and this causes conda-build to enter an infinite loop when it tries to install the earlier-built subpackage (in this case, building rust-pkg2 fails trying to install rust-pkg1).

The old solver presumably detected that it was hitting the exact same situation here and making no progress and well, I'm not sure, but somehow it worked.

To test, this should get you spinning:

```
conda-build rust-base rust-recommended
conda-build rust-pkg1 rust-pkg2 rust-pkg3 --no-test
```

What do you recommend here?

issue_title: conda env giving strange non-blocking behavior on Windows
issue_body: Using conda 4.4.4, this is happening in both the Anaconda prompt and Git Bash. To reproduce, simply run `conda env -h`. The prompt will return and it seems like conda is run in the background, producing output a few seconds later. Strangely enough, this doesn't happen when running `conda-env -h`.

issue_title: conda install --force implies --no-deps
issue_body:
This is a (breaking) change in behaviour  in 4.0.7. These two options should be connected the other way around (--no-deps implies --force), because there are use cases for --force with dependencies (for example an equivalent to update-or-install).

It is not very nice to have to update my scripts to check whether a particular package was installed first (and then call update) so as to restore the old behaviour, especially considering that it takes a few extra seconds.

issue_title: Creating a virtual environment starting with '.' (dot/period)
issue_body:
Hi,
Is there any way to create a virtual environment that starts with a '.' (dot/period)? I am running a program that searches for virtual python environments in a specific dot-prefixed folder name. The developers for that software use `virtualenv`  for their environment, but I would like to keep using conda and was wondering if there is a workaround for this.

Cheers

issue_title: conda uncontrollably spawning python processes when creating/removing envs
issue_body: On Windows 7, with Anaconda installed for all users in C:\Anaconda3. No problem creating and removing envs with admin rights. However, when running as user without admin rights, I get the following problem when creating environments: packages are downloaded ok, but then it hangs (usually at the linking or unlinking packages step) and I can see pythonw.exe processes being launched in task manager at a rate of several per second. Only way to stop this is to shut down the machine. The problem is intermittent and seems to occur more frequently when creating envs with all the anaconda packages (e.g. conda create -n test anaconda=4.1.1) rather than environments that only contain a few packages. Happens occasionally when trying to remove envs. I have tried reinstalling with multiple versions of anaconda (inc. the latest, 4.3.0) but the problem keeps occurring.

issue_title: Error in function Package.__lt__()
issue_body:
There are two problems in function `Package.__lt__()`

A. The code in [resolve.py:203](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/resolve.py#L203)
```python
            return ((self.norm_version, self.build_number, other.build) <
                    (other.norm_version, other.build_number, self.build))
```
should read
```python
            return ((self.norm_version, self.build_number, self.build) <
                    (other.norm_version, other.build_number, other.build))
```

B. When version numbers do not conform to [PEP 386](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0386/) (i.e. are not of the form `N.N[.N]+[{a|b|c|rc}N[.N]+][.postN][.devN]` with `N` representing natural numbers), the above comparison fails, and conda falls back to a lexicographic comparison between version strings ([resolve.py:206](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/resolve.py#L206)):
```python
            return ((self.version, self.build_number) <
                    (other.version, other.build_number))
```
This fall-back is a bad choice because it leads to unpleasant surprises when people are not aware of the concept of conforming version numbers. For example
```
1.0.9b < 1.0.10c => True  (both are conforming)
1.0.9b < 1.0.10d => False (1.0.10d is non-conforming)
```
I see two solutions:
* reject non-conforming versions numbers during `conda build`
* implement the fall-back comparison in terms of the rule 'numbers are compared numerically, strings of letters are compared lexicographically'

I highly favor the latter solution.

issue_title: Epoch option for age filter
issue_body:
I'd like to request a feature:

I think the current setting of an epoch in the age filter (as a point of reference) is not very practicable. An automated task would have to read / update / write the yaml settings file if it tried to change the epoch.

My use case is that I that the epoch for my age filter needs to be changed frequently, i.e. "snapshot all indexes prior to date x". That date is calculated in a script and can change frequently. It would be nice to have a feature to pass the epoch through a command line flag, or at least pass the whole action.yml file as a string to the curator command line.

issue_title: [FEATURE] allow `count` filter to operate on groups
issue_body:
add `pattern` parameter to `count` filter that would allow it to operate
within groups: given indices `[logs-1, logs-2, logs-3, metrics-1, metrics-2]`
the filter
```
- filtertype: count
  count: 1
  pattern: '^(.*)-\d$'
  reverse: true
```
will produce `[logs-3, metrics-2]` - a list composed of last index for
every prefix.

The motivation for this is to be able to identify `active` indices in a rollover index group.

issue_title: [FEATURE] Add alias option to Shrink action to allow aliasing original index name to newly created index after deletion of original index
issue_body:
The Shrink action provides a great way to process a batch of indices, shrink them to new indices and delete the originals.

It would be super handy to also have the option to add the alias for the original index name to the newly created shrunken index for each index processed.

Currently you have to either:

- Leave a bunch of duplicate indices around until the end of the batch then delete+alias, putting unnecessary pressure on resources depending on the size of the indices/batch...or
- run with missing indices until the end of the batch then add the aliases at the end of the batch

And with the alias+delete steps, these are also not atomic and it would be neater if Curator's Alias action also had the option to use the `remove_index` action provided by the Index Alias API:
https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/indices-aliases.html#CO146-3

issue_title: exists() should not return always True with ignore=404
issue_body:
When querying a missing document,
`get(index=index, doc_type=doc_type, id=id)`
raises an exception, and
`get(index=index, doc_type=doc_type, id=id, ignore=404)`
returns no result, as expected, without the exception.

But if
`exists(index=index, doc_type=doc_type, id=id)`
raises an exception, as expected,
`exists(index=index, doc_type=doc_type, id=id, ignore=404)`
returns True, i.e. the incorrect result.

Shouldn't `exists(ignore=404)` return the correct answer without the exception?

issue_title: Document "maxsize" (or equivalent) more prominently
issue_body:
I just had a scenario where I had to use Elasticsearch from a Tornado web service via its `ThreadPoolExecutor` integration. I was noticing terrible tail latencies on queries. After some digging, I discovered this was because of the `maxsize` parameter on the default connection class, [documented here](https://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.org/en/master/connection.html#urllib3httpconnection-default-connection-class). The issue was that though my thread pool supported up to 500 threads, it was limited by the 10 concurrent connections that the driver had set on the underlying pool.

Thankfully, I could fix this by passing "maxsize" to the `Elasticsearch` object, since this then passes through to the connection object. But given how big an impact this parameter has on performance in multi-threaded scenarios, I think the documentation should call it out much more prominently. People are probably running very slow code in production without realizing it, even if they have big ES clusters, as a result of leaving this default.

issue_title: Storage command arguments consistency
issue_body:
Please align all the arguments in storage commands so they are consistent. For instance when I want to download a blob; destination folder argument is **"-f"**; however in the file case it is **"--dest"**

```
az storage blob download -c $container_name -f $destination_folder/$blob_name -n $blob_name
az storage file download -s $share_name -p $source_path --dest $destination_path
```

Another example is that in the upload case, it is totally different:

```
az storage file upload --source $full_path_to_file -s $share_name -p $directory_name
az storage blob upload -f $file_to_upload -c $container_name -n $blob_name
```

Can we please simplify these to be **-d** for destination, **-s** for source ?

issue_title: PUBLIC NOTICE: Azure Storage Python Revamp
issue_body:
The Azure Storage team is taking ownership of the storage runtime library which is present in this repository and released as the azure-storage package on pypi. As promised in the [package change announcement](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/440), there will be many new storage features coming soon.

The first round of changes will be the largest and will bring the library up to the newest service version. As it currently stands, the library supports very few features which were released after 2012. To best support all the new features released on the service in the past 3 years, we will be making breaking changes to bring the Python storage library up to date. Some of these changes include append blob support, file service support, table batch refactors, replacing AtomPub with JSON in table, account SAS, improved response parsing and many other small features and API updates. 

All of this is being done in the dev branch and we will be maintaining ChangeLog and BreakingChanges files so you can see a summary of what’s happening. Note that this branch should not be considered stable!

After bringing the library up to the latest version we will do a preview release. Then we will begin adding additional improvements. These may include items such as built-in MD5 calculation, automatically following continuation tokens on list APIs, improved queue message encoding support, and many others, in addition to improvements the community suggests. 

We’d love to hear from you!

Thanks,
The Azure Storage Team

issue_title: Provide consistent behavior for 429 status code
issue_body:
If service sends 429 back, then we must look for retry-after header. If retry-after header is not available, then by default retry after 20 seconds. If retry-after header is available, then we must wait until the time provided by the service before retrying again. Make this a pluggable component so that if customers intend to add their own behavior for 429, they can.

From https://github.com/Azure/autorest/issues/2601

issue_title: Switch to new multidict version
issue_body:
Current `multidict` master has changed behavior: keys for `CIMultiDict` are always `str` (before they was either `str` or `istr`) and they are stored as-is without title casing. But key comparison is performed on title-cased basis.

Also `CIMultiDict` lookup is about 30% faster.

It is backward incompatible change, some aiohttp tests are failing. But all functionality is working on just released aiohttp 2.2.0 (it's crucial for smooth transition).
 
I need release new multidict first and fix failed aiohttp tests.

issue_title: Partial model validation of nested models
issue_body:
I would like to validate a partial model with `model.validate(partial=True)`. This works correctly for the immediate fields of the model, but not for the fields of nested models.

Note that this is only an issue in schematics v1.x, not in schematics v2.x. Unfortunately, in nearly all productive systems, I am still on v1.x due to the various API changes coming with v2.x.

Here is an example:
```python
from schematics.models import Model
from schematics.types import StringType
from schematics.types.compound import ListType
from schematics.types.compound import ModelType

class Thing(Model):
    id = StringType(required=True)
    value = StringType(required=True)

class Things(Model):
    things = ListType(ModelType(Thing))

# As expected, this will raise a ModelValidationError.
thing = Thing({"id": "1"})
thing.validate()

# As expected, this will not raise a ModelValidationError.
thing.validate(partial=True)

# The following should work, but it only works with schematics>=2.0.1, not with 1.1.3
things = Things({"things": [{"id": "1"}, {"id": "2"}]})
things.validate(partial=True)
```

I wonder if there is a way to fix this without too much effort in schematics v1.x.

Best!

issue_title: 'ipv4addr' field needed for dhcpmember in creating network for old NIOS
issue_body:
In older NIOS like 6.11.7 (WAPI version 1.6), 'dhcpmember' requires 'ipv4addr' field.

Here is how this is implemented in older IPAM driver.

create_network in object manager:

        members_struct = []
        for member in members:
            members_struct.append({'ipv4addr': member.ip,
                                   '_struct': 'dhcpmember'})
        network_data['members'] = members_struct

"ipv4addr" field of "member" list is mandatory in 6.12.x build, whereas it is optional in 7.3 build.

We will need to support this in object manager.

issue_title: Subdomain Registrar Registration API should be identical to Domain Registration
issue_body:
When registering a subdomain, a client should be able to POST the same data they would for a domain registration. The only difference in the schema is that a subdomain cannot be a fully qualified address (i.e., cannot contain a TLD).

The response code should also be the same (202) for valid registration attempts, though the message and error codes will be necessarily different.

issue_title: Warn on unreachable code:
issue_body:
It would be nice if pyflakes could warn on the following code:

```python
def foo():
    return 1
    print(1) # unreachable

def bar():
    raise Exception('oh no')
    print(1) # unreachable
```

Ideally, it would also cover cases like this:

```python
def foo(x):
    if x:
        return 1
    else:
        return 2
    print(1) # unreachable
```

and possibly even this:

```python
def foo(x):
    if False:
        print(1) # unreachable
```

(although I know mypy can use `if False: import foo` for type definitions that aren't used at runtime.)

Would you be willing to accept a patch to pyflakes that added this?

issue_title: Modular visualization JavaScript in the HTML file or separate?
issue_body:
Right now, the JavaScript to render each visualization module is included in the template HTML file as a script tag. This means that on the final page, each visualization div has its own separate script tag. This is ugly, and also not very standard for web development. But none of the alternatives are great:

- Include the code in a separate JavaScript template. This means that each module needs at least two external files: an HTML template, and a JavaScript template.

- Have users write template code directly in Python, as a string. This seems non-ideal, since it involves writing JavaScript in a Python environment, as a long and hard-to-debug string.

- Other ideas?

The modify command incorrectly checks value of "state" attribute when "session" attribute is passed in modify(). See line 217 in pool.py:

issue_title: RPM/DEB build automation
issue_body:
This issue is to address the creation of automation around building .rpm and .deb packages natively within the different operating systems that are supported.  This automation is to auto-handle the:

- The resolution of dependencies
  - stdeb.cfg (.deb building)
  - setup.cfg (.rpm building)
- Building of the packages
- The Testing of the packages

As such, this new solution should include a means by which to make a 'one source of truth' source file for all dependencies for a project.

Since this automation is a heavy lift, this automation should also be...

- Transferable to other projects and other github repos
- Modifiable and future proof

issue_title: Documentation confusion on keytabs
issue_body:
### What went wrong?
I think this is just a lack of understanding on my part, or a lack in the documentation, but I have been trying to understand how to specify a keytab to be used for the acquisition of the TGT. It appears you can use the KRB5_CLIENT_KTNAME environment variable, but this is undocumented (as far as I have found). 

You have a mention of keytabs in the basic tutorial (which I really appreciate someone having written up by the way):

> Note that for the krb5 mechanism, in order to acquire credentials with the GSSAPI, the system must already have a way to access those credentials. For users, this generally means that they have already performed a kinit (i.e. have cached a TGT), while for services (like above), having a keytab is sufficient. This process is generally performed outside the application using the GSSAPI.

But how do you specify the location? Is this part of the store variable: https://pythongssapi.github.io/python-gssapi/latest/gssapi.html#gssapi.creds.Credentials.store

I don't know, and I don't know how the dictionary for the store is supposed to look as there are no examples:

> store (dict) – the store into which to store the credentials, or None for the default store.

Are we looking at something like this: https://web.mit.edu/kerberos/krb5-1.12/doc/basic/ccache_def.html

Or am I totally missing the point?

issue_title: Product Structure and Attributes
issue_body:
Hi all

I've now been hard at work implementing our new shop in django oscar. Sadly django-oscar isn't very good at product variations and attributes. I'm considering how to fix this, and would like some input from the community.

Imo the best and simplest implementation would be to have every product be a variation, even if it's a standalone product. This greatly simplifies working with products all around the framework, and would make product editing in the dashboard more intuitive. You could even change the layout in the dashboard if only a single variation is created.

I've made a little list of things that the variation system should be able to handle.
- Variant generation from attributes set in product class
- No duplicate variants should be allowed
- Multi inline editing of variants
- Image on variants (for automatic image switching on product page)

Regarding Attributes i have some ideas as well. First order of business would be to unify Options and Attributes. It's confusing that these are 2 separate entities. This would entail removing the AbstractOption model, this is already confusing since the only way to add options is through django admin. There should be a global entry to manage attributes. Looking through the current models, it seems like much of this can be implemented right away, with a few modifications.  

and here is a list of the thing attributes should able to handle
- Attributes should have it's own global management, and then be attached to product classes
- Swatch type on the ProductAttributeValue (for handling frontend representation e.g. Color, Image etc.)

There's probably more stuff that i forgot in this writeup, so please let me know if missed anything and if you are interested in helping out implementing. Obviously much of the work will be in the frontend, and i think i read somewhere that the dashboard was about to be rewritten, so if anyone has information on this i would be glad to have it. 

Cheers

issue_title: Decide if reviews should exist without products
issue_body:
There's an [unresolved discussion](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/django-oscar/1gur_QmbgzY/W2_MOW906cMJ) about whether reviews should be deleted when the product is deleted. This issue has been added to track it.

If it is decided that reviews can live on their own, it should be ensured that the dashboard handles that case okay. The related issue #836 has been fixed now.

issue_title: Manual Authenticator mode might now work with some web configs Nginx that block dot directory/file access
issue_body:
In the client's Manual Authenticator mode the the directory the client is looking for the ACME challenge is prefixed with a dot

i.e. `.well-known/acme-challenge/ET_sy6y1KuIy1dk-vn8KfGyW_T20MHQ2ddcLFgMknys`

    Make sure your web server displays the following content at
    https://newdomain2.com/.well-known/acme-challenge/ET_sy6y1KuIy1dk-vn8KfGyW_T20MHQ2ddcLFgMknys before continuing:
    
However, certain Nginx configurations best practices are to block file and directory access to dot prefixed folders and files

    location ~ /\.          { access_log off; log_not_found off; deny all; }

So Manual mode instructions

    Make sure your web server displays the following content at
    https://newdomain2.com/.well-known/acme-challenge/ET_sy6y1KuIy1dk-vn8KfGyW_T20MHQ2ddcLFgMknys before continuing:
    
    ?*>|l0t6u$

issue_title: Support for multi-OS Apache
issue_body:
The Apache plugin has been designed and tested on the Ubuntu/Debian layout.  It shouldn't be that difficult to adapt the code to work with other OS configurations easily, but some work needs to be done.

Some initial ideas...

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/installer-throwing-error-message/1120/9?u=jdkasten

Also, failure should be graceful for unsupported layouts...

issue_title: Sensible behavior if storage.py is interrupted while updating a cert lineage
issue_body:
We should have some kind of lock or status flag that indicates which lineage, if any, is currently being updated.  Then if we get interrupted by an exception or signal during the process, we should check that lineage for consistency and delete it (if it's new) or roll it back (if it already existed) if the current state is inconsistent.

Right now it's very bad if a cert lineage ends up in an inconsistent state; it can cause the renewer to crash.

issue_title: Python3 VirtualEnv masks the required Python2 VirtualEnv
issue_body:
When running `letsencrypt-auto` using the recommended parameters on Ubuntu 14.04LTS, the script successfully installs its dependencies, but soon stops with this failure:

```
Creating virtual environment...
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 444, in _build_master
    ws.require(__requires__)
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 725, in require
    needed = self.resolve(parse_requirements(requirements))
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 632, in resolve
    raise VersionConflict(dist,req) # XXX put more info here
pkg_resources.VersionConflict: (virtualenv 13.1.2 (/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages), Requirement.parse('virtualenv==13.2.0.dev0'))

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/virtualenv", line 5, in <module>
    from pkg_resources import load_entry_point
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 2749, in <module>
    working_set = WorkingSet._build_master()
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 446, in _build_master
    return cls._build_from_requirements(__requires__)
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 459, in _build_from_requirements
    dists = ws.resolve(reqs, Environment())
  File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 628, in resolve
    raise DistributionNotFound(req)
pkg_resources.DistributionNotFound: virtualenv==13.2.0.dev0
```

I manually upgraded virtualenv with pip, but that only took it to v13.1.2 from v13.1.0, not the required 13.2.0.dev0.  Python version currently installed is 3.4.3.  I then updated the virtualenv installed for Python 2.7.6 on this system using pip2, and the script was able to complete.  It would seem that despite `letsencrypt-auto` checking Python3 by default, it is expecting to only work with Python2?

issue_title: Parsing compose IDs is (practically) impossible if `-` is allowed in product short name
issue_body:
I'm not sure if this is an issue or a cry for help...:)

Consider these compose IDs (one a real Fedora compose ID, one a made-up layered product compose ID from the test suite, since I don't know where to find a real RHEL layered product compose ID):

`Fedora-Atomic-25-20170205.0` (product short name: `Fedora-Atomic`)
`F-22-updates-BASE-3-updates-20160622.n.0` (product short name: `F`)

Now I read the code, all kinds of other subtleties are possible, which makes parsing compose IDs just super fun. But you could, I think, cope with many if not all of them by counting `-` characters. Maybe. I don't know. Life is awful.

However, as long as `-` characters are allowed in the product short name, all bets are off, because it just becomes entirely impossible to figure out which `-`s are being used as some kind of field separator by productmd, and which are just part of the product name (as in the first example).

I've been doing this to parse compose IDs in Fedoraland:

    (name, version, _) = compose_id.rsplit('-', 2)
    (date, typ, respin) = productmd.composeinfo.get_date_type_respin(compose_id)

but now I look at the test cases and code upstream, it's clear this is rather specific to my use cases and is relying on the fact that we don't have layered products (or at least, if we do, I don't care about any of 'em), or any `type_suffix`es (at least ones with dashes in them? I don't really know what these are), and I certainly don't care about RHEL 5 composes. But clearly someone does care about all those cases, so putting a generic compose ID parser in productmd (which is what I was trying to do) appears to be really rather futile, or at the least, would be very complex and have quite a bit of magic knowledge in it.

So...I don't know what to do. Would it be worthwhile making it a rule that product short names can't have dashes in them, and changing Fedora's configurations appropriately (to use `FedoraAtomic`, `FedoraCloud` and `FedoraDocker` as the short names, or something)? Or should I just give up on generically parsing compose IDs and rejig everything I have that does it, somehow (probably by requiring a round trip to the compose metadata or PDC, unfortunately)?

issue_title: Add in RFC7529 extensions to rrule.
issue_body:
Looks like [RFC7529](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7529) provides some significant extensions to the RRULE, including a more satisfactory resolution to #149.

We should probably implement these extensions. I haven't read it thoroughly, but we may want to see if these extensions allow `BYEASTER` to be easily recast as a combination of other rules. This will allow us to generate compliant `rrulestr` strings specified with the `byeaster` input.

issue_title: Distract the vector constructor and Vector type in parser
issue_body:
Most of the TL definitions use the `Vector t` type, but some of them (only one at the moment), use a `vector` constructor.

__Example:__
```cpp
// vector consructor:
future_salts#ae500895 req_msg_id:long now:int salts:vector<future_salt> = FutureSalts;

// Vector type:
messageActionChatCreate#a6638b9a title:string users:Vector<int> = MessageAction;
```
__Problem:__
Currently all vector arguments are being serialized as `Vector` (with uppercase V), so it is impossible to check the CRC32 of the string:

```php
hash('crc32b', 'future_salts req_msg_id:long now:int salts:Vector future_salt = FutureSalts')
=> "94e2d547"

hash('crc32b', 'future_salts req_msg_id:long now:int salts:vector future_salt = FutureSalts')
=> "ae500895" # Passes!
```
It's not a crucial issue, but anyway :no_mouth:

issue_title: Adding pickle.load and pickle.loads into cloudpickle namespace
issue_body:
I understand that you do not include an unpickler:
> [It does not include an unpickler, as standard python unpickling suffices.](https://github.com/cloudpipe/cloudpickle/blob/master/cloudpickle/cloudpickle.py#L9)

However it would be convenient if injecting the pickle `load()` and `loads()` methods into your name space, thus it can be used as a drop in.

You have `import pickle` surely it's almost a simple as:
```python
    load = pickle.load
    loads = pickle.loads
```

If I am honest, I am a little inconvenient to have to write:
```python
    try:
        import cPickle as pickle
    except ImportError:
        import pickle

    import cloudpickle
```
when I need to read and write pickled files/objects etc., and have to remember to use `cloudpickle.dump`[`s`]`()` and `pickle.load`[`s`]`()`.

Just having those functions in your namespace, just redirecting the work to `pickle` itself would make code cleaner, can therefore can be used as a drop in replacement:

```python
    import cloudpickle as pickle
```
This is common practise with an alternative pickler `dill`:
```python
    import dill as pickle
```
`dill` is great, but I have found that `cloudpickle` can pickle something I need that `dill` cannot.

I wouldn't mind even doing it myself if need be; however rather than doing the work (which may not be as simple as stated above), creating a pull request, and it being rejected because you do not want this feature; I thought I would ask first.

Is this something that you would be interested in doing yourselves, or accepting an pull request for? or am I barking up the wrong tree?

issue_title: Decide on a default schema for attributes
issue_body:
@slinnarsson, @gioelelm 

@pl-ki asked me what the default metadata attributes are that we use in the Loom files. He wants to make sure that his changes to the pipeline have the right values come out by default.

This is probably a good moment decide if we want to support an officially suggested "attribute schema", one that we also put in the loom documentation. 

I'm in favour of this for the following reasons:

- while _we_ can see that a row attribute labelled `Gene`, `GENE` or `GeneName` is probably the same thing (labels for gene names), it's still a lot easier to write scripts that work on random `.loom` files if there are default conventions for common attributes.
- `loom-viewer` currently supports certain default values. Making these match default conventions makes the usage of the viewer easier: people know what to expect, I can make it do more things automatically.

The proposed updated schema below is based on three things

- metadata attributes added by `loompy.create_from_cellranger()`
- conventions used by `loom-viewer`
- the proposal in #7. Note: for `loom-viewer`, there is only one row attribute that is currently *required* for it to work: "Gene", this proposal would remove that need.

## Global Attributes

- `title` (optional, `loom-viewer` uses the file name if absent)
- `description` (optional)
- `url` (optional)
- `doi` (optional)

Suggested additions, as explained in #7: `rowName`, `rowKey`, `rowSearchKey`, `colName`, `colKey` and `colSearchKey`.

### Reserved global attribute

- `creation_date` 

Added by default when a `.loom` file is first created by loompy, used to sort loom files in viewer. It's good to be able to track when a loom file was first created, and we shouldn't mess with the attribute afterwards either.

## Column attributes

- `CellID` (optional, added by `create_from_cellranger`)

Default attribute for `colKey` and `colSearchKey`

###  Reserved column attributes (do not use)

- `(original order)` 

Used to keep track of original column order in `loom-viewer`.

## Row attributes

- `Accession` (optional, added by `create_from_cellranger`)
- `Gene` (optional, added by `create_from_cellranger`)

Default attributes for `rowKey` and `rowSearchKey`

###  Reserved row attributes (do not use)

- `(original order)` 

Used to keep track of original column order in `loom-viewer`.

# Supported default values

The `loom-viewer` will automatically check for the presence of certain row and column attributes, and use them as default values in various views. These are all optional attributes.

## Default x/y attribute for column scatterplot:

- `_X`, `_Y` (added by `create_from_cellranger`)
- `X`, `Y` (legacy support for older loom files, not suggested as schema)
- `SFDP_X`, `SFDP_Y`
- `_tSNE1, `_tSNE2`
- `_PCA1, `_PCA2`

## Default colour attribute for column scatterplot, heatmap, and sparkline:

- `Clusters` (created by `create_from_cellranger`)
- `Class`
- `Louvain_Jaccard`
- `_KMeans_10`

## Default x/y attribute for row scatterplot:

- `_X`, `_Y`
- `X`, `Y` (legacy support for older loom files, not suggested as schema)
- `_LogMean`, `_LogCV`
- `_tSNE1, `_tSNE2`
- `_PCA1, `_PCA2`

## Default color attribute for row scatterplot, heatmap, and sparkline:

- `_Selected`
- `_Excluded`

By the way, we also discussed adding default gene lists to `loompy` at one point, what happened to that?

Also, the documentation mentions this:

> The following global attributes are reserved:
>    schema, a type annotation schema (JSON-formatted string)

Didn't we ditch the schema requirement, since the Loom viewer [automatically infers data types](https://github.com/linnarsson-lab/loom-viewer/issues/77)?

- [ ] update documentation
  - [ ] global attributes: `colName`, `colKey`, `colSearchKey`, and `rowName`, `rowKey`, `rowSearchKey`
  - [ ] default values for the above: "Cells", `CellId`, `CellId`, and "Genes", `Accession`, `Gene`
  - [ ] reserved list: `(original order)`
  - [ ] Default values
    - [ ] default x/y attributes
    - [ ] default colour attributes
  - [ ] remove `schema` from reserved list?

issue_title: Removing rows in bigger than memory datasets
issue_body:
I have a .loom file of 33694 rows (genes) x 632571 columns (cells), so keep in mind that this volume of data cannot fit into the available memory. I want to do some gene filtering and thus removing some of them. I know that for performance reasons, deleting rows directly is not allowed, so I tried to use the `loompy.scan()` for `axis=0`. Here's a minimal example:

```
import loompy
import numpy as np

ds = loompy.connect('my_data.loom', 'r')

cg_exp = ds.map([np.count_nonzero], axis=0)

genes = np.where(cg_exp > 2)[0]
for (ix, selection, view) in ds.scan(items=genes, axis=0):
    loompy.create_append('gene_filtered_ds.loom', view.layers, view.ra, view.ca)
```

This throws:

```
/home/user/anaconda3/envs/tensorflow/lib/python3.6/site-packages/h5py/__init__.py:36: FutureWarning: Conversion of the second argument of issubdtype from `float` to `np.floating` is deprecated. In future, it will be treated as `np.float64 == np.dtype(float).type`.
  from ._conv import register_converters as _register_converters
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "gene_filtering.py", line 10, in <module>
    loompy.create_append('gene_filtered_ds.loom', view.layers, view.ra, view.ca)
  File "/home/user/anaconda3/envs/tensorflow/lib/python3.6/site-packages/loompy/loompy.py", line 807, in create_append
    ds.add_columns(layers, col_attrs, fill_values=fill_values)
  File "/home/user/anaconda3/envs/tensorflow/lib/python3.6/site-packages/loompy/loompy.py", line 281, in add_columns
    raise ValueError(f"Layer {layer} has {matrix.shape[0]} rows but file has {self.shape[0]}")
ValueError: Layer  has 460 rows but file has 415
```

I can see two issues in my code:

1. the `loompy.create_append()` tries to append columns to the file, which is of course the wrong way to go,
2. it tries to match the rows, but the genes in each chunk are different (both as genes per se and as number of genes passed the QC).

I played around with the `loompy.scan()` since from the documentation it seems able to return batches of either rows or columns, however I couldn't find any good way to write the data on a file iteratively. Is there any solution to this?

issue_title: Authorization Methods Design Problem for Non-ORM Resources
issue_body:
### Description

Authorization class method signatures are like the following:

```
def read_detail(self, object_list, bundle):
        """
        Returns either ``True`` if the user is allowed to read the object in
        question or throw ``Unauthorized`` if they are not.

        Returns ``True`` by default.
        """
        return True
```

The signature is perfectly fine for model backed resources but not very good for non-orm resources where there is no QuerySet impl. at all. Because of this, it's being hard to apply filtering before actual SQL execution (i.e.: filtering objects which belong to a specific user)

issue_title: `repr()` on a `ModelChain` instance fails if `orientation_strategy = 'None'`
issue_body:
If `orientation_strategy` is set to `'None'` when creating a `ModelChain`, the attribute `self._orientation_strategy` is set to `None` in the orientation_strategy setter (from line 353).
In the `__repr__` method, just above, each attribute is printed thanks to the inner function `getmcattr(self, attr)`. And if the argument `attr` is `None`, then the function returns `None`.
Then, when building the returned chain, `None` is passed to the concatenation operator, which fails.
